mnemonics.title: S-U-R for ML Types
mnemonics.content: S-U-R: Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement - Remember: 'Students Usually Relax' during exam

mnemonics.title: Deep Learning Layers
mnemonics.content: D.E.E.P. = Deep networks have Extra Eloquent Processing - Each layer extracts increasingly complex features

mnemonics.title: ML Pipeline Flow
mnemonics.content: G.I.F.T.S. = Get Input Data, Find Features, Train Model, Show Test Data, Success! - Remember: ML is like giving GIFTS to your computer

mnemonics.title: Supervised vs Unsupervised
mnemonics.content: SUPERVISED = Teacher guides with answers (labeled data); UNSUPERVISED = Student explores alone (finds patterns without labels)

acronyms.usage: When computers learn from data without explicit programming
ML using neural networks with multiple layers (deep architectures)
Broad field where machines mimic human intelligence
Computing system inspired by biological brain neurons
Deep learning for sequential data (time series, text)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an organization chart: Input Layer (reception) → Hidden Layers (processing departments) → Output Layer (final answer). More hidden layers = deeper network
Think of a sieve or filter: raw data passes through, and the system keeps only important features (like sifting flour)
Picture a teacher-student scenario: Model (student) makes predictions, errors are calculated, weights are adjusted (learning), repeated until accurate
Visualize stacking transparent sheets: each layer adds more detail - Layer 1 = edges, Layer 2 = shapes, Layer 3 = objects

analogies.analogy: Teaching a child to recognize cats: You show many pictures of cats (training data), the child learns patterns (features), eventually recognizes new cats (prediction)
Unlike traditional ML (hand-crafted rules), deep learning is like the child figuring out cat features themselves - edges, whiskers, ears - through many examples
Each neuron is like a tiny decision maker: receives multiple signals, weighs them, fires output if threshold reached - like many small referees voting
Learning with an answer key - like practicing math problems with solutions at the back of a textbook
Sorting mixed Legos by color/shape without being told what colors exist - finding hidden patterns on your own
Training a dog with treats: actions that yield rewards are repeated (positive), those causing punishment are avoided (negative)
